caveats: a: BLS occupation evidence is not unique to NAICS 325314.
a: Generic process automation is included only where AI can substitute decisions or avoid hiring; already automated tasks are excluded.
rho: This is bounded judgment rather than observed industry adoption.
rho: Plants with modern automated loadout and ERP systems have less remaining opportunity than paper-driven operators.
e: The NAICS definition confirms mixing activity but not recurring-service revenue share or LMM independence.
e: Retail fertilizer sites may combine eligible blending with substantial noneligible product resale and application services.

s5: It measures age rather than intended sale, transferability, or transaction completion.
q: No public contract sample was available.
q: Capture depends on whether the firm sells advice and logistics with the blend or competes on a transparent toll fee alone.
d5: Aggregate fertilizer consumption does not isolate mixing-only establishments.
d5: Constant nutrient tons can generate different blending-service demand as formulations and number of field-specific recipes change.

o: The estimate excludes labor automation already captured in a and rho and focuses on whether the accountable operator remains required.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring seasonal or programmatic blend orders priced per ton, batch, or formulation, combining purchased straight fertilizers and micronutrients with ingredient, freight, handling, storage, and blending economics.
lens.excluded_activities: Upstream nitrogenous or phosphatic material manufacturers, compost producers, pure distributors that do no mixing, captive farm or integrated internal blending units, one-off spot traders, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market fertilizer blenders that repeatedly formulate and mix purchased nutrient ingredients to external customer, retailer, agronomist, or private-label specifications.

a.bridge: BLS defines mixing and blending operators as workers who set up, operate, or tend equipment and reports 10,570 such workers across chemical manufacturing groups including 3253 in May 2023. BLS also states that automated manufacturing processes reduce manual tasks for this occupation. I map order intake, recipe calculation, procurement, inventory allocation, scheduling, batch records, quality checks, dispatch, and customer service to AI-enabled substitution or avoided hiring, while keeping loading, equipment tending, cleaning, sampling, maintenance, and physical safety tasks largely unexposed. The occupation evidence covers all industries and broad chemical groups, so it may overstate or understate the exact wage mix of fertilizer-only plants.
caveats: BLS occupation evidence is not unique to NAICS 325314.
Generic process automation is included only where AI can substitute decisions or avoid hiring; already automated tasks are excluded.
a.change_evidence: Payroll-weighted task and time studies from independent fertilizer blenders, including current ERP, recipe, scale, and automated-loadout systems, would materially change the range.
a.rationale: Mixing plants combine physical handling with information-heavy custom formulation and seasonal coordination. AI can automate meaningful order-to-batch administration and assist inspection, while the bulk material itself still requires operators and equipment.

d5.bridge: USDA total fertilizer consumption measures nutrient volume, not constant-quality demand for third-party mixing. It reports US consumption reached 21 million metric tons in 2013, was 20 million in 2014 and stable through 2020, then fell 9.4% to 18.3 million in 2021; nitrogen, phosphate, and potash shares were broadly stable over 2006-23. I use this cyclical but mature consumption record as a near-flat anchor. The proxy can overstate mixing demand if farmers buy straight products or self-blend, or understate it if precision agronomy increases the number and complexity of customized blends.
caveats: Aggregate fertilizer consumption does not isolate mixing-only establishments.
Constant nutrient tons can generate different blending-service demand as formulations and number of field-specific recipes change.
d5.change_evidence: USDA ARMS application data, crop-acreage baselines, retailer blend-versus-straight sales, custom-blend order counts, precision-agriculture adoption, and qualifying firms' customer cohorts would materially change the range.
d5.rationale: Fertilizer remains essential to crop production and blending matches nutrients to local agronomic needs, but crop acreage, farm economics, nutrient-use efficiency, direct shipment, and consolidation limit structural growth. The base is approximately flat.

caveats: The NAICS definition confirms mixing activity but not recurring-service revenue share or LMM independence.
Retail fertilizer sites may combine eligible blending with substantial noneligible product resale and application services.
e.change_evidence: A firm-by-firm census of the injected LMM population using revenue by custom blending versus resale, repeat-customer rates, private-label contracts, ownership, and captive status would directly determine eligibility.
e.rationale: Mixing purchased ingredients to a buyer's formulation is inherently close to a repeat outsourced production service, and seasonal agronomic programs recur. Eligibility is reduced for commodity resale-heavy retailers, captive units, firms below or above the economic band, and businesses whose relationships are purely transactional.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 325314 ($558,249,280) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 325314 ($6,549,710,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1801 (margins.json[325]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Chemical (Specialty) EBITDA/Sales 18.01%); revenue/employee $679,854 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 325314); class <5: 128 firms, avg revenue/firm $868,406 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $156,400 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 64 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,803,031 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $504,826 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 54 firms, avg revenue/firm $5,365,074 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $966,250 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 93 firms, avg revenue/firm $22,418,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $4,037,482 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 29 firms, avg revenue/firm $67,516,621 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $12,159,743 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 19 firms, avg revenue/firm $101,399,474 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $18,262,045 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 93

caveats: Software may enable self-service ordering without eliminating the physical blender.
The estimate excludes labor automation already captured in a and rho and focuses on whether the accountable operator remains required.
o.change_evidence: Customer purchasing studies tracking shifts from third-party custom blends to straight products, on-farm blending, liquid injection, upstream preformulation, or autonomous retail systems would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: Most year-five customized blend quantity still requires an accountable operator to source, weigh, mix, test, store, document, and load physical nutrients. Some quantity can bypass the lens through straight-product purchases, on-farm or retailer self-blending, upstream preformulation, or direct application systems.

caveats: No public contract sample was available.
Capture depends on whether the firm sells advice and logistics with the blend or competes on a transparent toll fee alone.
q.change_evidence: Customer contracts and invoices showing blend fees, ingredient pass-through, quote cadence, rebates, minimum volumes, cost-down commitments, renewal behavior, and churn after price changes would materially change the range.
q.rationale: Local agronomic relationships, formulation accuracy, storage, rapid seasonal availability, and delivery reliability support retention. Transparent nutrient prices, per-ton quoting, farmer and retailer bargaining, annual competitive bids, and ingredient pass-through share savings with customers.

caveats: This is bounded judgment rather than observed industry adoption.
Plants with modern automated loadout and ERP systems have less remaining opportunity than paper-driven operators.
rho.change_evidence: Before-and-after studies at fertilizer blenders measuring touches per order, planner and dispatcher hours, blend errors, rework, on-time delivery, accepted recommendations, and headcount avoidance would materially change the estimate.
rho.rationale: Five years is sufficient to integrate AI with order entry, agronomic formulation, inventory, scheduling, dispatch, records, and maintenance workflows at relatively simple blending plants. Fragmented legacy systems, seasonal peaks, recipe liability, scale calibration, hazardous-chemical reporting, and workforce adoption keep implementation below full exposure.

s5.bridge: Census reports that 51% of responding US employer-business owners were age 55 or older in 2018. I translate this broad aging pressure into a five-year qualifying-control-transfer probability, reducing for family succession, cooperative or corporate ownership, closures, internal reorganizations, noncontrol deals, and businesses too dependent on an owner or adjacent retail operation to transfer cleanly. The all-industry proxy may still understate aging among rural independent fertilizer blenders, but no industry-specific owner-age measure was found.
caveats: The Census statistic covers all responding employer-business owners, not this industry or firm band.
It measures age rather than intended sale, transferability, or transaction completion.
s5.change_evidence: Owner-age and succession interviews across independent fertilizer blenders, plus five-year Axial, GF Data, PitchBook, and cooperative acquisition records, would materially change the estimate.
s5.rationale: The industry's local and independent operating profile makes succession plausibly relevant, but retirement is not synonymous with an arm's-length control sale and seasonal working capital or environmental diligence can impede deals.

principal_driver: Recurring local custom formulations create a coherent service model and an AI-addressable order-to-batch workflow.
principal_weakness: Transparent nutrient pricing and low labor intensity limit retained benefit even when workflow automation succeeds.
